server.name: lingo
server.description: Organizational context layer for AI development tools — maps planning terminology to code locations, enabling AI tools to understand your organization's unique language and workflows.

LINGO_GLOSSARY_PATH.description: Path to the glossary JSON file (relative to project root or absolute)

LINGO_ORG.description: Organization name for new glossary stores

LINGO_LOG_LEVEL.description: Logging verbosity: debug | info | warn | error

query_context.description: Search the organizational glossary for terms matching a natural-language query

get_term.description: Retrieve a specific glossary term by ID or name

add_term.description: Add a new term to the organizational glossary

remove_term.description: Remove a term from the glossary

list_terms.description: List all terms, optionally filtered or searched

find_by_file.description: Find terms associated with a specific file path

bootstrap.description: AI-powered cold-start: infer terms from codebase scan and PM tools

suggest_code_changes.description: Analyze term change impact and generate code modification suggestions

create_from_text.description: Reverse-flow: parse natural language text into structured PM items, optionally route through adapter

learn_from_pr.description: Learn organizational terminology from a GitHub Pull Request — extracts terms from PR title/description and maps to changed files

record_signal.description: Record a coupling signal for a glossary term to strengthen its term↔code mapping score

list_adapters.description: List all available PM and SCM adapters with name, type, and displayName

lingo://terms/{termId}.description: Retrieve a specific glossary term by ID

instructions: Lingo is an organizational context layer that maps planning terminology to code locations. Use the available tools to search, browse, and manage your organization's glossary of terms. Each term connects a planning/product concept (e.g., 'Sprint Velocity') to specific code locations. Start with 'bootstrap' for AI-powered cold-start discovery, or use 'add_term' to manually build your glossary.
